content.text: I need a way to reliably fetch a description for my command objects, using an explicit attribute if available but defaulting to the first sentence of the help documentation.

Since we want robust fallbacks, this function prioritizes a custom `self.description` field. If that is missing, it smartly parses the docstring to pull a concise description.

```python
def get_description(self):
        """
        Gets the description of the command. If its not supplied the first sentence of the doc string is used.
        """
        if self.description:
            return self.description
        elif self.__doc__ and self.__doc__.strip():
            return self.__doc__.strip().split('.')[0] + '.'
        else:
            return ''
```

This design ensures every command has some textual representation even if the user forgets to set an explicit attribute.
What happens if I have a long docstring but no periods in it?

If there are no periods, `split('.')[0]` will return the entire stripped docstring without modification. The code still ensures a period is appended for grammatical completeness outside of the split logic.
That makes sense; this will help make my command line interface much cleaner.
